diagrams.title: Minimum Distance Classifier System Architecture
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing the complete system architecture of the minimum distance classifier for pattern classification
elements: Input Image block - receives raw image data as input
Preprocessing block - handles resizing, normalization, noise reduction, and image enhancement
Feature Extraction block - extracts relevant features such as edges, textures, shapes, or statistical features from the preprocessed image
Feature Vector block - represents the extracted features as an n-dimensional feature vector (x1, x2, ..., xn)
Prototype Database block - stores pre-defined prototype feature vectors for each class (Class 1, Class 2, ..., Class M)
Distance Computation block - calculates distance between the input feature vector and each prototype using specified metric
Minimum Distance Selector block - finds the prototype with minimum distance to the input
Class Label Output block - assigns the class label of the closest prototype to the input image
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the end-to-end workflow of prototype matching classification, from raw image input to final class assignment. This shows how data flows through each processing stage.

diagrams.title: Feature Space Representation with Decision Boundaries
diagrams.description: Two-dimensional feature space visualization showing how different classes are separated by decision boundaries based on prototype positions
elements: X-axis labeled 'Feature 1' and Y-axis labeled 'Feature 2' representing two dimensions of the feature space
Three prototype clusters for different classes: Class A (blue circles), Class B (red squares), Class C (green triangles)
Prototype positions marked at coordinates representing mean feature vectors for each class
Linear decision boundaries shown as dashed lines forming polygon regions separating the three class regions
Test sample point shown as a black star at coordinates (3.2, 2.8)
Distance vectors shown as dotted lines from test sample to each prototype center
Shaded regions indicating classification zones where any point would be assigned to that class
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes how the minimum distance classifier creates decision boundaries in feature space and demonstrates that a test sample is assigned to the class of its nearest prototype. Students learn how geometric proximity determines classification.

diagrams.title: Prototype Matching Process Flow
diagrams.description: Detailed flowchart showing the step-by-step procedure for classifying an input pattern using prototype matching
elements: Start oval - beginning of classification process
Input Pattern parallelogram - receives the input image to be classified
Feature Extraction rectangle - extracts features from input image
Prototype Database cylinder - contains stored prototypes for all classes
For Loop rectangle - iterates through each prototype in the database
Distance Calculation rectangle - computes distance between input feature vector and current prototype
Comparison diamond - checks if current distance is less than minimum distance found so far
Yes/No branch from comparison - updates minimum if smaller, continues if not
End Loop rectangle - proceeds after checking all prototypes
Classification Result parallelogram - outputs the class label of prototype with minimum distance
Arrows showing sequential flow and decision paths with labels
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the algorithmic process of prototype matching, showing the iterative nature of distance calculation and decision making. Students understand the computational steps and logic flow.

diagrams.title: Distance Metrics Comparison Visualization
diagrams.description: Visual comparison of Euclidean and Mahalanobis distance metrics in feature space showing how each accounts for data distribution
elements: Scatter plot showing Class A data points with elliptical distribution (elongated horizontally)
Straight line connecting two points labeled 'Euclidean Distance' with formula d = √(Σ(xi-yi)²)
Elliptical contour lines around Class A centroid representing Mahalanobis distance with formula d² = (x-μ)ᵀΣ⁻¹(x-μ)
Covariance matrix representation showing variance in x-direction (σx²=2.5) and y-direction (σy²=0.8) with correlation ρ=0.6
Arrow showing Euclidean distance as simple geometric distance
Curved arrow showing Mahalanobis distance accounts for covariance (stretches in high variance direction)
Legend explaining that Mahalanobis normalizes for different scales and correlations
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the difference between Euclidean and Mahalanobis distance metrics. Students learn that Euclidean treats all directions equally while Mahalanobis accounts for the statistical distribution and correlations in the data.

diagrams.title: Minimum Distance Classifier Decision Regions
diagrams.description: Visual representation of decision regions created by minimum distance classifier for multiple classes in 2D feature space
elements: Feature space divided into Voronoi regions, each region shaded differently for Classes A, B, C
Prototype centers marked as stars at centroids of each region
Decision boundaries shown as perpendicular bisectors of lines connecting adjacent prototypes
Example test points at various locations showing which class each would be assigned to
Distance contour lines (concentric circles/ellipses) around one prototype showing equal-distance loci
Annotation showing that any point within a region is closer to that region's prototype than any other
diagrams.purpose: Students understand that minimum distance classifier creates Voronoi tessellation of the feature space, where each region contains all points closer to that class prototype than any other.

charts.title: Distance Metrics Comparison: Euclidean vs Mahalanobis
charts.content: Table with four rows comparing: (1) Formula - Euclidean is straight-line distance, Mahalanobis includes covariance inverse; (2) Assumptions - Euclidean assumes spherical clusters, Mahalanobis accounts for elliptical distributions; (3) Computational Complexity - Euclidean is O(n), Mahalanobis is O(n²) due to matrix operations; (4) Use Cases - Euclidean for equal-variance features, Mahalanobis for correlated features with different scales

charts.title: Classification Process Step-by-Step
charts.content: Vertical flowchart with 8 steps: (1) Acquire input image, (2) Preprocess image (noise removal, normalization), (3) Extract features from preprocessed image, (4) Form feature vector, (5) Load prototype vectors from database, (6) Calculate distance to each prototype, (7) Find minimum distance, (8) Assign class label of closest prototype. Includes decision diamond at step 6 asking 'Distance < Min?' with Yes/No paths.

charts.title: Types of Prototype Matching Methods
charts.content: Hierarchical chart showing: Prototype Matching divided into (1) Minimum Distance Classifier - uses mean prototype per class, computes distance to mean; (2) Nearest Neighbor Classifier - uses all training samples as prototypes, assigns to nearest; (3) k-NN - extends nearest neighbor by voting among k nearest prototypes. Further breakdown shows distance metric options for each.

charts.title: Minimum Distance Classifier vs k-NN Classifier
charts.content: Table comparing: (1) Storage - MDC stores one prototype per class, k-NN stores all training samples; (2) Computation - MDC computes distances to M prototypes, k-NN computes to N samples; (3) Decision Boundary - MDC creates linear boundaries, k-NN creates nonlinear boundaries; (4) Sensitivity to outliers - MDC is robust as it uses mean, k-NN is sensitive to outliers; (5) Training - MDC requires computing means, k-NN requires storing all samples.

branches: Prototype Matching Definition - technique to compare input with pre-defined class representatives
Minimum Distance Classifier - assigns class based on closest prototype using distance metric
Distance Metrics - Euclidean (straight-line), Mahalanobis (accounts for covariance), City-block (Manhattan)
Feature Extraction - process of converting image to feature vector representation
Feature Space - n-dimensional space where prototypes and test samples are represented as points
Decision Boundary - hyperplane separating classes based on minimum distance criterion
Training Phase - computing prototype vectors from labeled training samples (typically mean of each class)
Testing Phase - classifying new samples by finding nearest prototype
Applications - object recognition, image retrieval, gesture recognition, medical diagnosis
Advantages - simple, computationally efficient, works well with linearly separable classes
Limitations - sensitive to feature scaling, assumes spherical cluster shapes, one prototype per class limitation
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting all key concepts in prototype matching classification. Students can see relationships between mathematical foundations (distance metrics, feature space), implementation aspects (training/testing phases), and practical considerations (applications, advantages/limitations). This mind map helps integrate the topic with other computer vision concepts.
